items.prompt_md: Why does BFS, not DFS, guarantee that the first time you reach a cell is via the shortest path in an unweighted grid?
options: BFS explores all cells at distance 1 before any cell at distance 2, so a cell is first reached in strictly non-decreasing distance order
BFS marks cells visited less often than DFS
DFS cannot visit every cell in the grid
BFS always uses less memory than DFS
items.answer: BFS explores all cells at distance 1 before any cell at distance 2, so a cell is first reached in strictly non-decreasing distance order
items.explanation_md: BFS processes the graph in expanding "rings" of distance — everything one step away is fully processed before anything two steps away is even discovered, so the first visit to any cell happens at its true shortest distance.

items.prompt_md: In multi-source BFS (e.g. several rotten oranges spreading at once), why must every source be pushed into the queue before the first round of popping begins?
options: So that every cell's computed distance is measured from whichever source reaches it first, not from just one arbitrarily chosen source
So that the queue never needs to hold more than one element
So DFS can run afterward on the same queue
Because BFS cannot have more than one starting point otherwise
items.answer: So that every cell's computed distance is measured from whichever source reaches it first, not from just one arbitrarily chosen source
items.explanation_md: Seeding every source first makes "round 1" mean "one step from the nearest source" for every cell simultaneously — running BFS from one source at a time would give each cell its distance from that single source, not from whichever source is actually closest.

items.prompt_md: A multi-source BFS marks a fresh cell as visited/rotten only when it's popped from the queue, not when it's enqueued. What bug does this introduce?
options: The same cell can be enqueued multiple times by different neighbors before it's ever popped, wasting work and potentially miscounting rounds
The BFS will terminate before reaching every reachable cell
The queue will never contain more than one element
It converts the traversal from BFS into a valid topological sort
items.answer: The same cell can be enqueued multiple times by different neighbors before it's ever popped, wasting work and potentially miscounting rounds
items.explanation_md: If two different cells both discover the same unvisited neighbor in the same round, and visited status isn't set until dequeue, both will enqueue it — so mark cells the moment they're enqueued, not when they're eventually processed.

items.prompt_md: What does snapshotting the queue's length at the start of each round (`for _ in range(len(queue))`) before popping accomplish?
options: It isolates exactly the cells that belong to the current "wave," so a round counter can track something like minutes or steps correctly
It prevents the queue from ever growing during the round
It reduces the traversal from O(rows × cols) to O(rows + cols)
It performs a topological sort of the cells within the round
items.answer: It isolates exactly the cells that belong to the current "wave," so a round counter can track something like minutes or steps correctly
items.explanation_md: New cells discovered mid-round get appended to the same queue, so without freezing the round's size upfront you'd lose track of which cells belong to this round versus the next one — the snapshot is what lets one loop equal one round.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of a multi-source BFS over an R×C grid?
options: O(R × C)
O(R + C)
O(R × C × log(R × C))
items.answer: O(R × C)
items.explanation_md: Every cell is enqueued and dequeued at most once across the whole traversal, so total work is proportional to the number of cells.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of a multi-source BFS on an R×C grid, accounting for the queue and any visited tracking?
options: O(R × C)
O(log(R × C))
O(R + C)
items.answer: O(R × C)
items.explanation_md: In the worst case the queue (or the visited structure backing it) can hold on the order of every cell in the grid at once — for example, if nearly the whole grid is reachable in one wide wave.

items.prompt_md: A grid problem only asks "is any cell reachable from the start," with no mention of steps, time, or minimum count. Is BFS required over plain DFS?
options: No — plain DFS (or BFS) for reachability alone is enough; BFS's ring-by-ring structure only matters when you need the actual minimum distance
Yes — BFS is the only algorithm capable of visiting every cell
Yes — DFS cannot determine reachability in a grid
No — reachability requires Union-Find and cannot be solved by any traversal
items.answer: No — plain DFS (or BFS) for reachability alone is enough; BFS's ring-by-ring structure only matters when you need the actual minimum distance
items.explanation_md: BFS's defining advantage is that it discovers nodes in increasing distance order — if the question never asks about distance or timing, a plain DFS flood fill answers "reachable or not" just as well, and often more simply.

items.prompt_md: If a multi-source BFS finishes and some target cells were never reached at all, what does that mean for the answer to "minimum time until every cell is reached"?
options: There is no valid answer — the routine should signal that not everything was reachable (e.g. -1), since some cells never get reached by any source
The answer is simply the number of rounds that did run, ignoring the unreached cells
The BFS must be re-run using DFS instead
It means the grid contained a cycle
items.answer: There is no valid answer — the routine should signal that not everything was reachable (e.g. -1), since some cells never get reached by any source
items.explanation_md: If unreached cells remain after the queue empties, no amount of extra waiting would ever reach them, so the routine needs an explicit "unreachable" signal rather than reporting the round count as if everything succeeded.

items.prompt_md: Why is it valid to treat each round of a multi-source BFS as "one unit of time" (one minute, one step) rather than needing a separate distance-tracking array?
options: Because the grid is unweighted — every edge costs exactly one step, so "number of rounds popped so far" and "distance from the nearest source" are the same number by construction
Because BFS never needs to track distance at all
Because the grid always has exactly one connected component
Because DFS also guarantees this property
items.answer: Because the grid is unweighted — every edge costs exactly one step, so "number of rounds popped so far" and "distance from the nearest source" are the same number by construction
items.explanation_md: The one-round-per-step equivalence only holds because every edge in an unweighted grid costs the same — that's precisely the assumption that breaks if edges ever had different weights, which is when you'd need a weighted shortest-path algorithm instead.

items.prompt_md: Which phrasing in a problem statement is the strongest signal to reach for BFS over DFS on a grid?
options: "Find the minimum number of steps/minutes for something to spread or reach a target" — a shortest-path/shortest-time question on unweighted edges
"Count how many separate regions exist" — a pure connectivity question
"List the cells in the order a recursive call would visit them"
"Determine whether a valid ordering of tasks exists"
items.answer: "Find the minimum number of steps/minutes for something to spread or reach a target" — a shortest-path/shortest-time question on unweighted edges
items.explanation_md: "Minimum steps/time" on an unweighted grid is BFS's signature phrasing — pure connectivity favors DFS, and dependency-ordering questions point to topological sort instead.

items.prompt_md: A robot sits on an unweighted grid with some blocked cells. You need the minimum number of moves to reach a target cell. Which approach fits?
options: BFS for shortest unweighted path, expanding ring by ring from the start
DFS flood fill to enumerate every reachable cell
Topological sort of the grid cells
A monotonic stack over the rows
items.answer: BFS for shortest unweighted path, expanding ring by ring from the start
items.explanation_md: "Minimum number of moves" on an unweighted grid is exactly BFS's shortest-path guarantee — DFS would find *a* path, not the shortest one.

items.prompt_md: Several guard towers all start scanning outward simultaneously across a grid, one cell per minute. You need the minute at which every reachable cell has been scanned. Which approach fits?
options: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every tower before the first round
Single-source DFS from the first tower only
Union-Find merging every tower pairwise
A max-heap ordered by tower scanning range
items.answer: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every tower before the first round
items.explanation_md: Multiple simultaneous sources spreading at the same unit rate is the multi-source BFS fingerprint: seed all sources at once so each cell's round number reflects its nearest tower.

items.prompt_md: You can change one letter of a word at a time, and each intermediate result must be a valid word from a fixed dictionary. Find the shortest transformation sequence length from a start word to a target word. Which approach fits?
options: BFS over an implicit graph where words are nodes and one-letter edits are edges
DFS to enumerate every possible transformation
Topological sort of the dictionary words
Two pointers scanning the start and target words
items.answer: BFS over an implicit graph where words are nodes and one-letter edits are edges
items.explanation_md: "Shortest sequence of single-step transformations" is a shortest-path question over an unweighted graph of words — BFS finds the minimum step count; DFS would just find some valid sequence.

items.prompt_md: In a maze grid, you start at a given cell and must find the minimum number of steps to reach the nearest boundary/exit cell. Which approach fits?
options: BFS outward from the start until a boundary cell is popped
DFS flood fill counting total reachable cells
Topological sort of maze cells by distance
A hash map counting how many times each cell type appears
items.answer: BFS outward from the start until a boundary cell is popped
items.explanation_md: "Minimum steps to nearest exit" is a shortest-unweighted-path question, so BFS's ring expansion is the right tool — the first boundary cell popped is guaranteed to be the closest one.

items.prompt_md: In an unweighted social network graph, you must find the minimum number of introductions (hops) needed to connect two specific people. Which approach fits?
options: BFS from one person, stopping as soon as the other person is popped
DFS following one friend chain as deep as possible
A monotonic stack of people ordered by connection count
Backtracking over every possible chain of introductions
items.answer: BFS from one person, stopping as soon as the other person is popped
items.explanation_md: Minimum hop count between two nodes in an unweighted graph is the same shortest-path question as the grid case — BFS's level order gives the minimum directly.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array, you need to find the k-th largest element without fully sorting the array. Which approach fits?
options: A heap (min-heap of size k, or a max-heap) to track the top candidates
Multi-source BFS seeded from every array index
DFS flood fill over the array
Topological sort of the array values
items.answer: A heap (min-heap of size k, or a max-heap) to track the top candidates
items.explanation_md: "k-th largest/top-k" is a heap problem — there's no grid, adjacency, or spreading process here at all.

items.prompt_md: You're given the head of a singly linked list and must reverse it in place. Which approach fits?
options: Iteratively re-point each node's `next` pointer while walking three pointers (prev, current, next) down the list
BFS spreading outward from the head node
DFS flood fill marking each node visited
Topological sort of the list nodes
items.answer: Iteratively re-point each node's `next` pointer while walking three pointers (prev, current, next) down the list
items.explanation_md: Reversing a linked list is a pointer-rewiring problem specific to the linked-list pattern — there's no shortest-path or connectivity question being asked here.

items.prompt_md: You must check whether a string of brackets like `([{}])` is properly balanced and nested. Which approach fits?
options: A stack that pushes opening brackets and pops/matches on each closing bracket
BFS layer by layer over the characters
Multi-source BFS from every opening bracket
Union-Find merging matching brackets
items.answer: A stack that pushes opening brackets and pops/matches on each closing bracket
items.explanation_md: Bracket matching is the classic stack pattern — push on open, pop-and-check on close — with no grid or graph traversal involved.

items.prompt_md: A grid problem gives multiple starting cells representing an infection spreading simultaneously and asks for the minimum time until a specific target cell is infected, or -1 if that's impossible. Which approach fits?
options: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every infected cell and stopping once the target is popped
DFS from a single randomly chosen infected cell
Topological sort of the cells by infection order
Binary search over the number of minutes
items.answer: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every infected cell and stopping once the target is popped
items.explanation_md: Multiple simultaneous starting points spreading at a uniform rate through unweighted cells, with a minimum-time question, is exactly the multi-source BFS setup from this lesson.

items.prompt_md: A grid represents a building on fire: several rooms start on fire at once and fire spreads to adjacent open rooms each minute. Find the minute by which every reachable room has caught fire. Which approach fits?
options: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every already-burning room
DFS from a single randomly chosen burning room
Topological sort of the rooms by fire spread order
Binary search over the number of minutes
items.answer: Multi-source BFS, seeding the queue with every already-burning room
items.explanation_md: Multiple simultaneous ignition points spreading at a uniform rate through unweighted cells is exactly the multi-source BFS setup from this lesson.
